question: On the retry-heavy tasks T4 (HTTP 429) and T5 (HTTP 500), does the Robustness score depend more on (a) context window size, or (b) an explicit EVENTS scratchpad prompt instruction?

conditions.name: A: 8k context, default prompt
conditions.note: Silent retries: the agent retries mechanically but the retry events never appear in run_log, so the Robustness sub-scorer cannot verify fault handling. 8k context is truncated before the narration lands.

conditions.name: B: 128k context, default prompt
conditions.note: Same prompt, same model family, larger context. Robustness jumps from 0 to 12 because the retry narration now survives context and lands verbatim in run_log. No prompt engineering required.

conditions.name: C: 128k context, enhanced EVENTS prompt
conditions.prompt_variant: enhanced (persistent EVENTS scratchpad + 'log before you retry' framing)
conditions.note: Adding an explicit EVENTS scratchpad instruction on top of 128k produced ZERO additional improvement on T4/T5. The Robustness ceiling at 12/15 appears to be a sub-criterion the judge applies (e.g. exact retry-count match or timing fidelity) rather than a narration-quality gate.

deltas.verdict: Context window dominates prompt engineering on this benchmark. Adding explicit scratchpad instructions is neither necessary (8k was the binding constraint, not the prompt) nor sufficient (128k + better prompt does not push past the Robustness ceiling).

implication: For benchmarks with long tool-use trajectories, context-size selection should come BEFORE prompt engineering. A clean way to think about it: if the observation log cannot fit in context, no amount of instruction to 'log carefully' will recover the lost narration. Practitioners optimising agents on ComtradeBench-style tasks should first size the model to the episode length, then invest in prompt engineering only to push above the natural ceiling. The remaining 3/15 gap on T4/T5 Robustness is a structural property of how the judge scores retry events — future work (see Limitations).
